conversation.content: Write a recursive algorithm to compute the n-th Fibonacci number and analyze its runtime.

A classic recursive algorithm for computing the \(n\)-th Fibonacci number is based directly on the mathematical definition:

\[
F(n) =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } n = 0 \\
1 & \text{if } n = 1 \\
F(n-1) + F(n-2) & \text{if } n > 1
\end{cases}
\]

## Recursive Algorithm

```python
def fibonacci(n):
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    if n == 1:
        return 1

    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)
```

## Example

To compute \(F(5)\):

\[
F(5) = F(4) + F(3)
\]

Then:

\[
F(4) = F(3) + F(2)
\]

\[
F(3) = F(2) + F(1)
\]

and so on, until the base cases \(F(0)\) and \(F(1)\) are reached.

The recursion tree grows rapidly because the same subproblems are solved repeatedly.

For example:

- \(F(3)\) gets computed multiple times
- \(F(2)\) gets computed even more times

---

# Runtime Analysis

Let \(T(n)\) represent the runtime.

The recursive relation is:

\[
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1)
\]

This recurrence behaves similarly to the Fibonacci sequence itself.

The number of recursive calls grows exponentially, giving:

\[
T(n) = O(2^n)
\]

A tighter bound is:

\[
T(n) = O(\varphi^n)
\]

where

\[
\varphi \approx 1.618
\]

is the golden ratio.

## Why is it inefficient?

Because many values are recomputed repeatedly.

For example, computing \(F(6)\):

```text
F(6)
├── F(5)
│   ├── F(4)
│   │   ├── F(3)
│   │   └── F(2)
│   └── F(3)
└── F(4)
```

Notice:

- \(F(4)\) is computed twice
- \(F(3)\) is computed three times
- Smaller values repeat even more

This repeated work causes exponential growth.

---

# Space Complexity

The deepest recursive chain is:

\[
n \rightarrow n-1 \rightarrow n-2 \rightarrow \dots
\]

So the recursion depth is \(n\).

Therefore:

\[
\text{Space Complexity} = O(n)
\]

due to the call stack.

---

# Final Complexities

| Complexity Type | Value |
|---|---|
| Time Complexity | \(O(2^n)\) |
| Space Complexity | \(O(n)\) |

This recursive approach is elegant and easy to understand, but inefficient for large \(n\). Dynamic programming or iteration can reduce the runtime to \(O(n)\).
